revision.text:
[[commons:Category:Inada-jinja (Kasama)]][[ja:稲田神社]] {{Expand Japanese|稲田神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Inada Shrine | native_name = 稲田神社 | image = Inada-jinja (Kasama) haiden.JPG | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Inada Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|36.368635|140.206419}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Kushinadahime|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|de|Kushinadahime|en|Kushinadahime|es|Kushinadahime|fr|Kushinada-hime|it|Kushinadahime|ja|クシナダヒメ|mni|ꯀꯨꯁꯤꯅꯗꯍꯤꯃꯦ|ru|Кусинада-химэ|simple|Kushinadahime|sr|Кушинадахиме|zh|櫛名田比賣|qid=Q505469|1=Kushinadahime}} }} {{nihongo|'''Inada Shrine'''|稲田神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Hitachi Province]]. == instance of (P31) == * {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} * {{ill|Shikinaisha|lt=Shikinaisha|en|Shikinaisha|zh|式內社|qid=Q134917286|1=Shikinaisha}} * {{ill|Q9610964|ltq=Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}} * {{ill|Q135160342|ltq=Q135160342|qid=Q135160342|1=Kokuhei-sha}}Kokugakuin University Shrine database * {{ill|Q135026601|ltq=Q135026601|qid=Q135026601|1=Shikinai Hiteisha}} == part of (P361) == * {{ill|List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province|lt=List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province|ja|常陸国の式内社一覧|qid=Q11481683|1=List of Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province}} ** Series ordinal: 21 ** P155: {{ill|Q135039767|ltq=Q135039767|qid=Q135039767|1=Ihafuneno Shrine}} ** P156: {{ill|Sashinono Shrine|lt=Sashinono Shrine|qid=Q135039768|1=Sashinono Shrine}} == located in the administrative territorial entity (P131) == * {{ill|Q845851|ltq=Q845851|qid=Q845851|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}} * {{ill|Q1055353|ltq=Q1055353|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}} * {{ill|Niihari district|lt=Niihari district|ja|新治郡 (常陸国)|qid=Q11502682|1=Niihari district}} == coordinate location (P625) == * {{coord|36.368635|140.206419}} == image (P18) == * Inada-jinja (Kasama) haiden.JPG == dedicated to (P825) == * {{ill|Kushinadahime|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|de|Kushinadahime|en|Kushinadahime|es|Kushinadahime|fr|Kushinada-hime|it|Kushinadahime|ja|クシナダヒメ|mni|ꯀꯨꯁꯤꯅꯗꯍꯤꯃꯦ|ru|Кусинада-химэ|simple|Kushinadahime|sr|Кушинадахиме|zh|櫛名田比賣|qid=Q505469|1=Kushinadahime}} == described by source (P1343) == * {{ill|Q135037878|ltq=Q135037878|qid=Q135037878|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database (old)}} ** P2699: https://web.archive.org/web/20131104202545/http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/206003.html == Kokugakuin University Digital Museum entry ID (P13677) == * 181774 ** P3831: {{ill|Kokugakuin University Shrine database|lt=Kokugakuin University Shrine database|qid=Q135159299|1=Kokugakuin University Shrine database}} == modern shrine ranking (P13723) == * {{ill|Q90139226|ltq=Q90139226|qid=Q90139226|1=Ken-sha}} ** End time: {'time': '+1946-02-02T00:00:00Z', 'timezone': 0, 'before': 0, 'after': 0, 'precision': 11, 'calendarmodel': 'http://www.wikidata.org/entity/Q1985727'} ** P1534: {{ill|Q3029647|ltq=Q3029647|qid=Q3029647|1=Shinto Directive}} == legal form (P1454) == * {{ill|religious corporation|lt=religious corporation|ja|宗教法人|qid=Q7311343|1=religious corporation}} == country (P17) == * {{ill|Q17|ltq=Q17|qid=Q17|1=Japan}} == postal code (P281) == * 309-1635https://www.houjin-bangou.nta.go.jp/download/zenken/index.html == Corporate Number (Japan) (P3225) == * 3050005002694 == Commons category (P373) == * Inada-jinja (Kasama) == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '茨城県笠間市稲田763', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Inada-jinja (Kasama)}} [[JA:稲田神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11596799}} == Japanese content == {{Shinto shrine |Name = Inada Shrine |Image = [[File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) haiden.JPG|280px]]
Haiden (worship hall) |Location = {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kasama City|Q845851|qid=Q845851|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}} Inada 763 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{ill|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|Q505469|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinada-hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}} |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha|1=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = Solid zelkova wood {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} style, copper roofing |Other names = Inadahime Shrine / Himenomiya“The Gods of Japan,” entry on Inada Shrine. |Pilgrimage sites = |Annual festival = {{ill|lt=November 17|Q3028|qid=Q3028|1=November 17}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki|label=Inada Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} {{List of coordinates}} [[File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) torii.JPG|thumb|200px|right|Torii gate]] '''Inada Shrine''' (Inada-jinja, '''稻田神社''') is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} located in Inada, {{ill|lt=Kasama City|Q845851|qid=Q845851|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}), and under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=former shrine ranking system|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} it was classified as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Enshrined deities === '''Principal enshrined deity''' * '''{{ill|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinadahime|Q505469|qid=Q505469|lt=Kushinada-hime no Mikoto|1=Kushinadahime}}''' (くしいなだひめのみこと) '''Associated enshrined deities''' * {{ill|qid=Q1107286|lt=Futsunushi|Q1107286|qid=Q1107286|lt=Futsu-no-Kami|1=Futsunushi}}, {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}, {{ill|qid=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui|Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|qid=Q10935105|lt=Ōyamakui no Mikoto|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}, Amateru Ōmikami, one unidentified deity *: All five were enshrined together in {{ill|lt=Meiji era|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}})The explanatory sign on the shrine grounds lists only Kushinada-hime no Mikoto as the enshrined deity. The five associated deities are based on “The Gods of Japan,” entry on Inada Shrine.. === History === === Founding === The date of founding is unknown. According to the “Inadahime-no-Miya Shrine Origin” ({{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), when a servant of the local village headman Takemochi went to draw water from the Inada Yoshii spring, a woman appeared beside the spring. When Takemochi went to ask about her after being informed by the servant, she replied, “I am Kushinada-hime, the local tutelary deity of this land.” She then delivered a divine message instructing that shrines be built for her parents and for her as a married couple, and that rice be cultivated with the water of the Yoshii spring and worshipped accordingly. About 300 meters northwest of the shrine, on the mid-slope of Mount Inada, stands the main sanctuary (Okunoin). To the left of its small shrine, a massive rock juts out; this {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}} is said to be the site where Inada-hime descended to earth. It is also believed that the shrine was worshipped by the {{ill|lt=Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11502674|qid=Q11502674|1=Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}. The Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko were {{ill|lt=kuni no miyatsuko|Q2483673|qid=Q2483673|1=Kuni no miyatsuko}} who are said to have ruled the {{ill|lt=Niihari District|Q5765974|qid=Q5765974|1=Niihari District, Ibaraki}} area before the {{ill|lt=Ritsuryō system|Q742460|qid=Q742460|1=Ritsuryō}}. In the “{{ill|lt=Sendai Kuji Hongi|Q2716751|qid=Q2716751|1=Kujiki}}” under the “{{ill|lt=Kuni no Miyatsuko Honki|Q133833194|qid=Q133833194|1=Kokuzo Hongi}}” entry for Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko, it is recorded that Hinarafu no Mikoto, son of Mitoroki no Mikoto, was the first to hold the office. Mitoroki no Mikoto (also read Mitsurogi no Mikoto) was an eighth-generation descendant of {{ill|qid=Q10940685|lt=Ame-no-Hohi|Q11395856|qid=Q11395856|1=Ame no Hohi}} and shared ancestry with the {{ill|lt=Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11395856|qid=Q11395856|1=Izumo clan}}. In the area around this shrine are several {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}—{{ill|lt=Sashino Shrine|Q27500771|qid=Q27500771|1=Sashino Shrine}}, {{ill|lt=Kamo Ōkami Miko Kannushi Tama Shrine|Q66085127|qid=Q66085127|1=Kamo-Ōkami-miko-kami-nushi-tama Shrine}}, and {{ill|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (Sakuragawa City)|Q11433665|qid=Q11433665|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine|1=Okunitama Shrine}}—all of which enshrine deities of Izumo lineage, leading scholars to point out connections with the historical records. === Overview === There are no references to this shrine in official histories such as the {{ill|lt=Six National Histories|Q1151168|qid=Q1151168|1=Rikkokushi}}. However, in the mid-{{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, the “{{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}}” {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Register of Deities|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} lists “Inada Shrine, Myōjin Taisha” in {{ill|lt=Hitachi Province|Q1055353|qid=Q1055353|1=Hitachi Province}}, {{ill|lt=Niihari District|Q5765974|qid=Q5765974|1=Niihari District, Ibaraki}}, ranking it as a {{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|qid=Q9610964|1=Myōjin Taisha}}. In the early {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|qid=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, the local lord {{ill|lt=Kasama Tokitomo|Q11599988|qid=Q11599988|1=Tokitomo Kasama}} invited eight poets including {{ill|lt=Hamuro Mitsutoshi|Q23613350|qid=Q23613350|lt=Fujiwara no Mitsutoshi|1=Hamuro Mitsutoshi}} and {{ill|lt=Utsunomiya Yasutsuna|Q11449785|qid=Q11449785|lt=Yasutsuna|1=Yasutsuna}} to hold a poetry offering gathering at the shrine. The poems from this event are recorded in the “Shin Wakashū” (Utsunomiya Shin Wakashū). At the end of the {{ill|lt=Muromachi period|Q334845|qid=Q334845|1=Muromachi period}}, the shrine buildings were destroyed by warfare. In {{ill|lt=Keichō|Q1328185|qid=Q1328185|1=Keichō}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1602|Q6727|qid=Q6727|1=1602}}), when {{ill|lt=Ina Tadatsugu|Q3387913|qid=Q3387913|1=Ina Tadatsugu}} conducted a land survey of the area, the main hall and other structures were rebuilt“Place Names of Ibaraki Prefecture,” entry on Inada Shrine.. In {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|qid=Q1146228|1=Kanbun (era)}} 8 ({{ill|lt=1668|Q7595|qid=Q7595|1=1668}}), the domain lord Inoue Masatoshi granted the shrine four koku of tax-exempt land. In {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1694|Q7694|qid=Q7694|1=1694}}), {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|qid=Q1194944|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}} visited the shrine, lamented the decline of the ancient sanctuary, and dedicated origin records and other items (the shrine treasure of the Four Guardian Deity Banners was dedicated in Genroku 11 ({{ill|lt=1698|Q7706|qid=Q7706|1=1698}})). The shrine buildings burned down again in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|qid=Q1325317|1=Kōka}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|qid=Q7636|1=1845}}) and were rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Kaei|Q1146125|qid=Q1146125|1=Kaei}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1848|Q7647|qid=Q7647|1=1848}}). In the {{ill|lt=Meiji era|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}}, under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, the shrine was designated a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Prefectural shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}. === Shrine grounds === File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) honden.JPG|Main hall File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) sandou.JPG|Approach path === Subsidiary and auxiliary shrines === '''Shrines within the grounds''' * Tenazuchi Shrine (てなづちじんじゃ) ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Q643763|qid=Q643763|lt=Tenazuchi no Mikoto|1=Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi}} *: The “father shrine” of the “parents’ shrine” mentioned in the origin legend; located to the front left of the worship hall. * Ashinazuchi Shrine (あしなづちじんじゃ) ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi|Q643763|qid=Q643763|lt=Ashinazuchi no Mikoto|1=Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi}} *: The “mother shrine” of the “parents’ shrine” mentioned in the origin legend; located to the front right of the worship hall. * Yakumo Shrine ** Enshrined deity: {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} *: The “husband shrine” of the “married couple shrine” mentioned in the origin legend; located to the right of the main hall. In addition, several other shrines stand within the grounds. File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) Tanazuchi-jinja.JPG|Tenazuchi Shrine File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) Ashinazuchi-jinja.JPG|Ashinazuchi Shrine File:Inada-jinja (Kasama) Yakumo-jinja.JPG|Yakumo Shrine '''Shrines outside the grounds''' * Main sanctuary (Okunoin) *: Located about 300 meters northwest of the main shrine on the mid-slope of Mount Inada ({{Coord|36|22|15.59|N|140|12|12.91|E|region:JP-08_type:landmark|name=Main sanctuary (Okunoin)}}). It is said to be the shrine’s former original site. === Festivals === * First prayer ceremony (January 1)Festival information based on explanatory signs within the shrine grounds. * Setsubun Festival (February 3) – held on the 4th in leap years * Kinen Festival (February 19) * Gion Festival (July 30) *: A grand festival based on the legend that Susanoo no Mikoto reunited with Kushinada-hime no Mikoto at the site of the main sanctuary; the portable shrine spends one night at the Okunoin. * Grand annual festival (November 17) === Cultural properties === === Tangible Cultural Property designated by Ibaraki Prefecture === * Four Guardian Deity Banners (4 streamers) (historical materials) *: Dedicated in {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11 ({{ill|lt=1698|Q7706|qid=Q7706|1=1698}}) by {{ill|lt=Tokugawa Mitsukuni|Q1194944|qid=Q1194944|1=Tokugawa Mitsukuni}}. They depict the Four Guardian Beasts: Azure Dragon (East), White Tiger (West), Vermilion Bird (South), and Black Tortoise (North). In the lower right of the banners is the inscription: “Respectfully dedicated Four Guardian Banners, Jōshū Niihari District Inada Shrine, Genroku year, Minamoto Mitsukuni.” Designated on January 25, Showa 63 (1988)[http://www.city.kasama.lg.jp/index.php?code=331 Four Guardian Deity Banners] (Kasama City website).. === Visitor information === '''Address''' * Inada 763, {{ill|lt=Kasama City|Q845851|qid=Q845851|1=Kasama, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}} '''Access''' * {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|qid=Q499071|1=East Japan Railway Company}} (JR East) {{ill|lt=Mito Line|Q302388|qid=Q302388|1=Mito Line}} {{ill|lt=Inada Station|Q6013353|qid=Q6013353|1=Inada Station}} (about 15 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Explanatory signs within the shrine grounds * {{Cite book|ja|editor=Meiji Shrine Historical Materials Compilation Office|author=|year=1912|chapter=Inada Shrine|title={{ill|lt=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō|Q11486103|qid=Q11486103|1=Fuken Gōsha Meiji Jinja Shiryō}}|publisher=Meiji Shrine Historical Materials Compilation Office|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Meiji Shrine Historical Materials|1912}}}} ** [{{NDLDC|1088244/597}} “Meiji Shrine Historical Materials: Fuken Gōsha, Upper Volume”] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see frames 597–598. * “Encyclopedia of Japanese Historical Place Names: Ibaraki Prefecture” ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|qid=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Kasama City, entry on Inada Shrine * Kitabatake Katsumi, “Inada Shrine,” in {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken’ichi|Q2392596|qid=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}} (ed.), “The Gods of Japan – Shrines and Sacred Sites – 11 Kantō” ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|qid=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}})) === Related items === * {{ill|lt=Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Q11502674|qid=Q11502674|1=Niihari no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} === External links === {{Commonscat|Inada-jinja (Kasama)}} * [http://www.ibaraki-jinjacho.jp/ibaraki/kenou/jinja/03116.html Inada Shrine] (Ibaraki Prefecture Association of Shinto Shrines) * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/206003.html Inada Shrine] (Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program “Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Database”) {{Shinto portal}}{{List of Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Inada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Inada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Inada Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:笠間市の歴史]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hitachi Province]]
